coredata.dc:title: Self-powered Vs. high speed ZnO-based photodetectors
coredata.dc:creator: Mousavi, S. Soraya
Sajad, Batool
coredata.dc:description: A poly (vinyl alcohol) matrix incorporating ZnO-GQD composite structures was used in a photodiode configuration as a novel kind of nanocomposite. The crystal structure and morphology of the composites were studied in depth. The electro-optical properties of the fabricated photodiodes using the composites were interpreted based on the intrinsic characteristics of the composites. The ZnO/PVA-based photodiode offers self-power with approximately high specific detectivity of 4.6 × 1010 Jones at zero bias. However, graphene quantum dots provided numerous advantages, particularly improving the response speed of the photodiode ZnO-GQDs/PVA dispossessed the ability due to introducing GQDs. A high-speed response (0.06 s, at least three times faster than ZnO/PVA photodiode) was recorded for ZnO-GQDs/PVA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite structure
Graphene quantum dots
Self-power photodiode
Polymeric photodiode
Fast response photodetectors
Flexible PVA-based composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g. technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support), but who do not meet the criteria for authorship, are named in the Acknowledgements and have given us their written permission to be named. If we have not included an Acknowledgements, then that indicates that we have not received substantial contributions from non-authors. This statement is signed by all the authors (a photocopy of this form may be used if there are more than 10 authors):

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A Comparison between XRD patterns of (a)ZnO and ZnO-GQDs, and (b) ZnO/PVAand ZnO-GQDs/PVA composites, (the main planes (100), (002), and (101) are associated with the hexagonal phase of ZnO).
XRD patterns of (a) GQDs, and (b) pure PVA.

SEM micrographs of (a) ZnO particles, (b) ZnO-GQDs composite structures, (c) TEM image of GQDs, (d) TEM micrograph of the nanocomposite composed of ZnO-GQDs (the inset represents a ZnO particle surrounded by GQDs). (e) and (f) represents the films prepared using ZnO-GQDs/PVA.

I-V plots photodiodes of (a) A comparison between darkcurrents of two photdiodes, (b) photocurrents of ZnO/PVA photodiode, (c) and (d) photocurrents of ZnO/GQDs/PVA photodiodes for different wavelengths.

I-t plots photodiodes of (a) A comparison between responsivities of ZnO/PVA photdiode at different bias voltages, (b) A comparison between I-t curves of two photodiodes at zero bias, (c) and (d) I-t curves of both photodiode at different voltages.
A comparison between Cj of ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQDs/PVA photdiodes at forward bias.
Schematic structure of the fabricated device.

(a) Non-polar surface of ZnO-O terminated (1 0 1 0) before filling in PVA [25], (b) Polar (1 0 0 0) surface after compositing with PVA, (c) ZnO/PVA composite, interaction between Oxygens of O-terminated ZnO and Hydrogens of PVA.
The full width at half maximum of the ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQD/PVA nanocomposites and their fillers.
A comparison between the specifications of the fabricated photodiode based on ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQD/PVA nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Self-powered Vs. high speed ZnO-based photodetectors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Faculty of Physics and Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, Faculty of Physics and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Faculty of Physics and Chemistry, Alzahra University, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A poly (vinyl alcohol) matrix incorporating ZnO-GQD composite structures was used in a photodiode configuration as a novel kind of nanocomposite. The crystal structure and morphology of the composites were studied in depth. The electro-optical properties of the fabricated photodiodes using the composites were interpreted based on the intrinsic characteristics of the composites. The ZnO/PVA-based photodiode offers self-power with approximately high specific detectivity of 4.6 × 1010 Jones at zero bias. However, graphene quantum dots provided numerous advantages, particularly improving the response speed of the photodiode ZnO-GQDs/PVA dispossessed the ability due to introducing GQDs. A high-speed response (0.06 s, at least three times faster than ZnO/PVA photodiode) was recorded for ZnO-GQDs/PVA.

ce:section.ce:para: Along with the increasing development of photonic technologies, the need for fabricating accurate and high-sensitivity optical detectors is progressively felt. Photodetectors measure the intensity of different wavelengths depending on their bandwidth. They are widely used in various fields such as imaging, spectroscopy, telecommunication, motion detection, chemical sensing, aerospace and military applications, and bio-medical [1–5]. The rapid growth of application diversity leads to high demands for innovative technologies that can improve the photodetectors' speed, efficiency, wavelength range, and material flexibility. Recently, the promotion of flexible photodetectors has been accelerated by the molecular design of nanocomposites. The unique and tunable characteristics of nanocomposites make them of great importance to polymer-based materials [6]. The novel polymeric materials comprising at least one nanoscale component attract enormous attention for sensing purposes [7–10]. The optical properties of the composites contain semiconductor nanostructures that can be tuned/controlled by tailoring their size, shape, and surface capping [11]. One of the best fillers in optical nanocomposites is Zinc oxide (ZnO). With a wide bandgap of 3.37 eV and a high exciton binding energy of 60 meV, ZnO has been widely used in flexible blue-emitting polymer-based composites [12].

Moreover, ZnO is environmentally friendly, biocompatible, and easily fabricated by facile synthesis routes. It can be shaped into various nanostructures (e.g., nanocrystal, nanowire, nanopillar, and nanobelt) [13,14]. Thus, ZnO nanocrystals or quantum dots are an ideal building block for fabricating short-wavelength modern electro-optical devices. Composites containing ZnO are gaining interest due to their need in optoelectronics industries, especially organic photodetectors. Among the different kinds of host polymers, polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a highly biocompatible, non-toxic, synthetic, and water-soluble one with appropriate film-forming properties that can be used as the host of composite material [15]. ZnO/PVA, an antibacterial composite with high blue fluorescence intensity, has been widely utilized as a light-emitting composite [16]. Up to now, research on flexible self-powered U.V. detectors has been carried out in many groups. On the other hand, many publications address using various graphene features to improve the optical and electrical characteristics of polymers [17–20]. Investigations indicated that the composition of ZnO and graphene quantum dots with their reinforced characteristics could be exploited as the active materials for fabricating high-performance UV photodetectors.
The ZnO-GQDs/PVA nanocomposite is prepared through the synthesis method described in Ref. [9].

ZnO nanoparticles are synthesized through the sol-gel route as described previously according to Ref. [21]. Zn (CH3COO)2·.2H2O (99.5% Merck), Trimethylamine (TEA) (99% Merck), and absolute ethanol (99% Merck) were used as the precursor, surfactant, and solvent, respectively. The zinc ions solution was prepared with a molar concentration of 1 mol/L. For this aim, zinc acetate dehydrate was dissolved in a mixture of ethanol and TEA under vigorous magnetic stirring. The molar ratio of TEA to zinc acetate dehydrate was adjusted to 2:5. After obtaining the homogeneous and transparent solutions, they were kept at room temperature for a while to complete precipitation. Then, the white precipitates were dried at 120°C for one h in an oven to vaporize the solvent. Finally, the prepared powder was annealed in a furnace at 800°C for two hours.

Graphene quantum dots were synthesized through the method suggested by Dong et al. [22]. The GQDs were prepared by directly pyrolyzing citric acid. According to the procedure, 2g citric acid (99%, Sigma Aldrich) was put into a 5 mL beaker and heated to 200 °C. After 30 min, the obtained orange liquid was neutralized to pH 7.0 by adding NaOH solution (100 mg/L) under vigorous stirring. The neutralized liquid was then dialyzed with a 6-8 kilo Dalton dialysis tube for 24 h.

For this aim, 0.1 gr as-synthesized ZnO nanoparticles were added to the 5 ml as-dialyzed GQDs suspension. It was stirred for 2 h to be adequately mixed. The mixture was then dried at 50 ˚C overnight in an oven. The obtained powder was then annealed at 150 ˚C for an hour.

Initially, a PVA solution was prepared by adding 0.5 gr PVA (99%, Merck) to 50 ml DI water to prepare the nanocomposite thin films. The mixture was stirred vigorously at 85 ˚C for 5 h until a clear solution was achieved. The sols were prepared with an optimized amount of ZnO-based dopant (4 wt. %) [9,23]. Then, ZnO and ZnO-GQDs powders were added to the PVA solution and mixed for 2 h. They were then ultrasonicated repeatedly to obtain the uniformly dispersed solutions of ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQDs/PVA.

For this aim, p-type silicon wafers were used as the substrate and cleaned according to the RCA-1 regulation. Then, the composite films were deposited by spin coating, as described in our previous work [9]. After that, the electrical contacts were created through a simple lithography step and using the Ag paste. To generate the contact electrode on a silicon wafer, a photo mask was used with a rectangle shape of 3×10 mm2. Then, the general sequence of the photolithography process was performed as follows: photoresist spin coating, prebaking, exposing, post-exposure baking, and developing. Shipley 1813 (S1813) and Tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAH) was used as the photoresist and developer solution. In the next step, Ag paste was used as the metallic contact. Finally, contact structures were patterned completely through a photoresist lift-off process using acetone. Scheme 1 depicted the schematic structure of the fabricated devices composed of ZnO-GQDs (inset).

In this research, we comprehensively discuss the various features of both types of photodetectors by studying their materials characteristics. First, the composites' crystal and structural quality were analyzed for this aim. At last, the operation of the polymeric photodetectors was evaluated under different wavelengths and voltages. The other electrical feature of the samples were also measured for accurate studying.

X-ray diffraction patterns of the ZnO particles, ZnO-GQDs composites, PVA, ZnO/PVA, and ZnO-GQDs/PVA nanocomposite films are plotted in Fig. 1 . Among the three polymorphs of ZnO, the hexagonal wurtzite (P63mc), zinc blende (F
4 ¯
3m), and cubic or rock salt (Fm
3 ¯
m), the wurtzite phase is stable, and the latter two are metastable [24]. The wurtzite phase of ZnO particles with sharp peaks was confirmed at the standard positions (Fig. 1(a)).

The three main peaks are located at the angle (2θ) of 31.61, 34.31, and 36.11, which correspond to (100), (002), and (101) planes of the ZnO. Fig. 1(a) shows that wrapping ZnO nanoparticles with GQDs preserves the peak position and improves its crystal structure.

The chemical bonds created between GQDs attached groups and ZnO defects amend the interfacial defects of ZnO nanoparticles and enhance their crystallinity. Due to the FTIR spectrum of GQDs (discussed in the supplementary), a high concentration of O-H bonds adjoins GQDs core structure. Therefore, it can reform the surface dangling bonds of ZnO nanoparticles related to its intrinsic defects such as oxygen vacancies (investigated in the P.L. spectrum, see supplementary).

Furthermore, a peak broadening in the XRD patterns of GQDs was observed that could be assigned to their small sizes (As TEM images confirmed). Subsequently, wrapping ZnO with these fine particles composed of chemical groups containing oxygen components leads to a higher intensity of ZnO-GQDs peaks than ZnO ones. It should also be noted that the pattern of pure PVA films presents two broad peaks at 19.5◦ and 40.4◦, demonstrating its semi-crystalline nature (Fig. 2 (b)). According to the studies, the intra/intermolecular hydrogen bonds established this semi-crystal structure of PVA [25]. Its peak intensity and crystallite size correspond to a measure of PVA chains crowded with each other. The amplitude of the PVA peak at the (1 0 1) plane decreases after adding ZnO. It is related to a decrease in diffraction on the (1 0 1) plane [26].

Moreover, a comparison between the pure ZnO and ZnO/PVA composite patterns reveals that the preferred orientation of the ZnO crystal structure has been altered after accompanying PVA. Thus, ZnO particles' preferred (1 0 1) orientation has turned to the (1 0 0) plane after interacting with PVA. ZnO has two polar and non-polar crystal faces due to the anisotropy characteristics of the wurtzite crystal structure. The polar faces are nominally detected as Zn- or O-terminated. The latter contain equal numbers of Zn and O. It should be noted that these surfaces reconstruct to lower the surface energy and adsorbing –O.H. groups at the Zn-terminated face and –H at the O-terminated ones [24].

As a result of the interaction of ZnO particles with PVA, the structure of ZnO converts to a polar one, as Scheme. 2 depicted. The polarity significantly affects surface configuration, stability, reactivity, nucleation and growth mechanisms, impurity, dopant incorporation, electro-optical properties, and related device performances [27]. Moreover, the XRD spectra of PVA-based composites filled with ZnO and ZnO-GQDs indicate different patterns (Fig. 1(b)). Adding GQDs to its structures served its crystallite properties, and one can see the preferred direction preserved in the ZnO-GQDs/PVA composite.

Nevertheless, the ZnO/PVA pattern possesses higher intensity peaks with a distinctive crystal. The XRD pattern of the sample composed of ZnO-GQDs exhibits an amorphous structure thoroughly. Introducing GQDs has highly disarranged the whole composite crystal characteristics. Although the prominent ZnO peaks still appear in the pattern, their low intensity denotes lower amounts of ZnO. The high intensity of a broad peak at the angle (2θ) of about 20˚ states the higher contents of GQDs. It is due to the existence of a wide and intense peak around this angle recorded for GQDs in its XRD pattern.

The FWHM measurement can provide meaningful insights into the surface state of materials, as grain distortion, dislocation density, and residual stress are related parameters. Table 1 listed the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the main peaks detected in the crystal structures of the composites and their components. Comparing the ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQDs/PVA FWHM of the diffraction peaks confirms the lax in crystallinity of the GQDs-based composite. XRD peaks are widening due to stacking faults and structural disorder. Increasing the hardness and density of point defects increases the grain boundary mobility, thereby extending the FWHM of the XRD peak linearly. The presence of stress in the material can also affect the FWHM [28].

The broad XRD peaks indicate the formation of small-size GQDs. The manifest alteration between the crystal structure of ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQDs/PVA composites occurs due to the increased hydrogen bonding between GQDs and the molecular chains of the PVA polymer. So the strong interaction of GQDs chemical groups with the side chain groups of PVA, mainly with the hydroxyl group, produces a molecular rearrangement and reorders the amorphous chains of PVA in another way [17].

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the structures formed by accompanying ZnO particles and GQDs are illustrated in Fig. 3 . The nanocomposites include the ZnO particles in the range of 100–200 nm surrounded by a 2.5 nm mean size of GQDs. The formation of the composite structures can be identified according to the SEM images. Surrounding the ZnO particles with graphene quantum dots is observable. The mean size of the particles increases significantly after wrapping with this transparent membrane composed of GQDs. However, this notable size enhancement occurs during the composite preparation process and aggregation of the particles. The morphology and surface features of the nanocomposite films are also presented in Fig. 3 (c) and (d) micrographs.

The main features of the photodiodes (dark and photocurrents) for the different wavelengths are plotted in Fig. (4) . The light sources were five light emission diodes (LEDs) ranging from 350 to 450 nm at 500 μW/cm2. The responsivity (R), external quantum efficiency (EQE), and specific defectivity (D*) as the primary considerable factors are calculated as below:
(1)
R =
I p
P
i n
× A
(2)
E Q E =
h c × R
e λ
(3)
D *
=
R
( 2 q
J d
)
1
/
2

Where Ip, Pin, h, c, and Jd are photocurrent, incident power, Planck's constant, velocity of light, and dark current density, respectively [28,29]. According to the Figure, the photocurrents of ZnO-GQDs/PVA photodiode are higher than those of ZnO /PVA. Likewise, its dark current is lower for a forward bias and slightly higher for a reverse bias.

Interestingly, the ZnO/PVA photodiode operates precisely as a p-n junction photodiode. As a result, the measured dark and photocurrents in one direction (forward bias) can be at least one order of magnitude higher than reverse bias. Moreover, they function as self-powered photodiodes based on their energy band structure. The depletion region created at the interface of n-type nanocomposite/p-type Si due to the carrier diffusion under thermal equilibrium conditions enhances the built-in electric field. It provides a driving force for the separation of the photo-created charges. The zero-bias also offered a decent detectivity of up to 4.6 × 1010 Jones, which can be explained by the high intrinsic potential and wide depletion width of the Schottky junction.

Generally, as organic photodetectors have a large bandgap (>1 eV), bulk thermal generation within the active layer makes a limited contribution to dark current. Still, charge injection may not be negligible under the induced reverse bias voltage, as in ZnO-GQDs/PVA photodiode [30].

Adding graphene derivatives to these types increases charge injection and, as a result, dark current under a reverse bias voltage. Accordingly, the ZnO-GQDs/PVA display a high dark and disordered current in the photovoltaic mode (V=0). In addition, the GQDs introduce electrons with high mobility, which lowers the barrier between the composite components. The disordered photo- and dark currents in photovoltaic mode can also be attributed to the non-crystalline structure of ZnO-GQDs/PVA composites with many defects, dangling bonds, and charge carriers discussed previously.

The smaller width of the depletion region and lower built-in electric field created due to the higher density of charge carriers in the absence of applied voltage is responsible for the random dark current [31].

The mean dark currents at V=0 for ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQDs/PVA photodetectors recorded about 0.2 nA and 8 nA, respectively. The trend reversed for the higher voltage bias, particularly at forwarding bias, whereby the lower dark currents were measured for ZnO-GQDs/PVA photodiodes compared to ZnO/PVA. At V=5 volt, the recorded dark current for ZnO/PVA photodiode was 12.3 µA, at least 17 times higher than that of ZnO-GQDs/PVA (0.7 µA). Moreover, the functionality of the photodetectors at zero bias was studied using their I-t curves and calculating their rise and fall times. The fall and rise times are defined as the time it takes to switch from 10% at one level to about 90% at the second level.

Almost all the results for all the wavelengths were approximately the same at V=0. Fig. 5 (b) depicted the response of the ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQDs/PVA at 0-volt bias under the illumination of 375 nm and 425 nm (wavelengths with the highest responsivity at V=5 volt), respectively. As seen, the response of the ZnO/PVA indicates that the pulses are well defined and on/off levels are stable and predictable. However, ZnO-GQDs/PVA device does not have any accurate response to the signals and just random amounts of currents recorded for this sample at V=0. The XRD and P.L. (Supplementary) analysis of the ZnO-GQDs/PVA indicates that many defects exist in the structure of this device which can be considered the source of the random dark current and noise of the photodetectors. R, EQE, and D* of the heterojunction device with an illumination intensity of 0.5 mW/cm2 were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 5, the responsivity of the ZnO/PVA increases distinctly as a function of the wavelength for three bias voltages. Table 2 summarizes the performances of both photodetectors and compares their functionality in photovoltaic mode and 5V bias. It can be seen that although the ZnO/PVA heterojunction device operates moderately at photovoltaic mode, the response time and detectivity of the ZnO-GQDs/PVA under forwarding bias improved significantly. In particular, its response speed is at least one-third of the ZnO/PVA photodiode. On the other hand, ZnO-GQDS/PVA cannot operate well with zero bias. But, ZnO/PVA offered EQE of 33%, the responsivity of 0.1 A/W, and 4.6 × 1010 Jones in zero bias voltage.

We can improve the photodiode's functionality by examining its basic electrical properties using the equivalent circuit model. Of course, there are not the same components in the photodiode models. However, in addition to the normal pn junction indicated by the diode symbol, four elements appear consistently: a current source, a parallel capacitor, a parallel resistor, a series resistor, and.

Cj refers to the junction capacitance of the photodiode, which is associated with its depletion region. The junction capacitance strongly influences the frequency response of the photodiode. Junction capacitance consists of two major components: depletion layer capacitance (Cdl) and diffusion capacitance (Cdiff). In a reversed biased p-n junction, a charge accumulation or depletion at the edge of the D.C. space charge region creates due to the ionization/ deionization processes related to both donor and acceptor impurities in the depletion layer, increasing depletion layer capacitance. This capacitance is directly related to the bias voltage and other junction parameters by the Eq. (4):
(4)
C
d l
=
A
(
V i
± V )
1
/
2
(
q
ε
N A
N D
2 (
N A
+
N D
)
)
1
/
2
with Vi standing for the built-in voltage, V is for the external bias voltage, and the remaining symbols are used as usual. The signs represent reverse and forward conditions, respectively.

On the other hand, forward biasing of a p-n junction leads to an additional contribution to Cj, which appears as the rearrangement of the minority carriers injected into the neutral zones. As a result, diffusion capacitance is expressed for low and high frequencies as follows:
(5)
C
d i f f
=
q I τ
2 K T
(
ω τ < < 1
)
(6)
C
d i f f
=
q I
K T
(
τ
/
2 ω
)
1
/
2
(
ω τ
> > 1
)

In both expressions, I and τ are the forward bias current across the junction and the average minority carrier lifetime. Then, the whole term for Cj, at the forward bias voltage, is:
(7)
C j
=
C
d l
+
C
d i f f

Accordingly, at higher frequencies, Cj tends to get the Cdl amount. Then, the C-ƒ curves are plotted for various frequencies and forward bias voltages because diffusion and depletion layer capacitances contribute to the junction capacitance [32]. The measurements were conducted to compare the electrical features of two sets at a constant forward bias (2V) within the frequency range of 100 Hz to 100 kHz at ambient temperature. Fig. 6 compares the junction capacitance of ZnO/PVA and ZnO-GQDs/PVA photodiodes. According to the figure, recorded Cj for the sample composed of GQDs assigns much lower amounts. The short response time of ZnO-GQDs/PVA photodiode corresponds to higher electron mobility of GQDs electrons and lower junction capacitance. Therefore, the lower junction capacitance leads to a shorter time constant (τ = RC), and a reduction in structure resistivity and capacitance is the reason for considerably shorter response time, as in other research [33,34].

A shunt resistance (RSH) is a resistor in parallel with a photodiode. The current-to-voltage ratio changes more noticeably as the shunt resistance approaches the load resistance. Noise performance is also affected by shunt resistance. The Johnson noise of a photodiode increases as RSH decreases [35,36]. RSH for ZnO-GQD/PVA photodiode calculated equal to 20.32 MΩ, while that is about 3.98 MΩ for ZnO/PVA photodiode. A higher shunt resistance permits smaller photocurrents measured in sensitive measurements. Moreover, the higher shunt resistance of ZnO-GQDs/PVA photodiode leads to lower amounts of Johnson noise, but increasing the RSH frequency reduces significantly. In addition to high current gains, a high shunt resistance allows for low voltage gains, as the results confirmed [37].

The series resistance (RS) determines by its contacts, wire bonds, and semiconductor material. Excessive series resistance can significantly reduce linearity. Various methods are used to calculate the series resistance, e.g., slope, Castañer, Luque, and other routes. We calculated the Rs as described in the literature , [38]. The measured Rs values of 2.5 and 12.5 Ω were recorded for ZnO-GQDs/PVA and ZnO/PVA photodiodes. The creation of ohmic contacts and lower resistance of the composite containing GQDs is responsible for its lower series resistance than ZnO/PVA, as was expected. The photocurrent passing through R.S. creates a voltage drop that begins forward-biasing a photodiode operating at zero bias. A forward-biased diode has an exponential current-voltage relationship. Consequently, the increasing voltage across R.S. reduces the photocurrent. The higher photocurrents and responsivity of the ZnO-GQDs/PVA sample can also be attributed to its lower Rs than the ZnO/PVA.

ZnO-GQDs/PVA can be exploited in many fields, including biomedical applications and optoelectronic devices. ZnO-GQDs have been dispersed in the polymer matrix to form highly functionalized polymeric films applicable to LEDs, displays, sensors, etc. This paper studied the effects of inserting GQDs into the composite on improving the photodiodes features. Addition of graphene to the composite results in a faster response and a lower dark current under a slight bias (5 volts). The disordering of the crystallinity, fluctuations and disordered currents at photovoltaic modes are the undesirable consequences of using GQDs, such that ZnO/PVA composite can be utilized for fabricating a self-power photodiode.
None.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g., technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support), but who do not meet the criteria for authorship, are named in the Acknowledgements and have given us their written permission to be named. If we have not included an Acknowledgements, then that indicates that we have not received substantial contributions from non-authors. This statement is signed by all the authors (a photocopy of this form may be used if there are more than 10 authors):
